statement: Multiple studies have assessed the potential impact of EU membership on Icelandic agriculture. The Icelandic Chamber of Commerce's 2012 assessment concluded that full CAP integration without special provisions would reduce Icelandic farm incomes by 30–50%, with sheep farming most severely affected due to loss of tariff protection and lower CAP direct payments relative to Iceland's current subsidy levels. A 2011 University of Iceland study estimated that consumer food prices could fall by 15–25% under EU membership due to tariff removal, benefiting consumers but threatening farm viability. The Farmers Association (Bændasamtök Íslands) has consistently argued that EU membership would be 'devastating' for Icelandic agriculture without permanent derogations. Pro-EU analyses counter that CAP structural funds and rural development programmes could offset income losses and modernise the sector.
source_name: Viðskiptaráð Íslands — Assessment of EU accession negotiations (2012); Hagfræðistofnun Háskóla Íslands; Bændasamtök Íslands position papers
caveats: These studies predate the current CAP (2023–2027) and were produced during the 2010–2013 negotiation period. Actual impacts would depend on negotiated terms (transition periods, national aid provisions, rural development allocations). Both sides of the debate have incentives to present extreme scenarios. Iceland's agricultural sector has also evolved since 2012.

related_entries.statement: Iceland's agricultural sector contributed approximately 1.0–1.2% of GDP in 2024 and employed around 3,500 people (roughly 1.7% of the labour force). The sector is dominated by sheep farming (approximately 450,000 sheep), dairy production (approximately 30,000 cattle), and greenhouse horticulture (using geothermal energy). Iceland has approximately 2,300 farms, with average farm size significantly smaller than the EU average.

Icelandic agricultural subsidies totalled approximately ISK 20–22 billion (roughly €130–145 million) per year as of 2024, primarily through direct payments to sheep and dairy farmers. This equates to approximately 55–60% of gross farm revenue being derived from government support, making Iceland one of the most heavily subsidised agricultural sectors in the OECD. The OECD Producer Support Estimate (PSE) for Iceland was 54% in 2023, compared to the EU average of approximately 19%.

Finland and Sweden secured permanent national agricultural aid provisions under Article 142 of the 1994 Act of Accession. This allows them to grant long-term national aid to maintain agricultural activity in northern regions with harsh climatic conditions, low population density (<10 persons/km²), and limited agricultural area (<10% of total land). Finland's Article 142 aid totals approximately €290 million per year (roughly 90% of its total national agricultural aid), covering per-animal payments for cattle, sheep, and horses, per-litre payments for milk, hectare-based crop payments, and greenhouse horticulture support. The current authorisation (2022–2027) caps total aid at €574.5 million per year, with a maximum of €221 million for cow's milk. Sweden has a parallel scheme. Austria received separate transitional and structural aid provisions. These are the closest precedents to what Iceland might negotiate.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Hagfræðistofnun Háskóla Íslands vann skýrslu sem sýndi fram á að ekki væri hægt að ganga í ESB á þeim forsendum að gera sérstakan samning um samruna Íslands og ESB.
Hagfræðiskýrsla Hagfræðistofnunar frá 2014 benti á að enn ætti eftir að láta á það reyna hvað raunverulega væri hægt að fá með samningum við ESB.

citations.report_title: ESB aðild hefði veruleg neikvæð áhrif á hagsmuni íslenskra bænda

claims.claim_text: Samtöl við bændur og samtök þeirra í ESB-löndum sýna óánægju með málefni landbúnaðarins innan sambandsins.
Bændasamtökin hafa ekki komið auga á nein sérstök tækifæri fyrir heildarhagsmuni ísleńsks landbúnaðar með ESB-aðild.
Galopnun landamæranna á innflutningi á landbúnaðarafurðum mun að öllum líkindum hafa veruleg neikvæð áhrif á hagsmuni íslenskra bænda.
ESB-aðild myndi leiða til galopnunar landamæranna á innflutningi matvöru og landbúnaðarafurða til Íslands.

citations.report_title: Landbúnaðarstefna ESB henti íslenskum landbúnaði illa

claims.claim_text: Landbúnaður er einn sá málaflokkur sem yrði fyrir umfangsmestu og varanlegum breytingum við ESB-aðild Íslands.
Frjálst flæði landbúnaðarvara hentar illa íslenskum aðstæðum.
Frjálst flæði landbúnaðarvara hentar illa íslenskum aðstæðum.
Sameiginleg landbúnaðarstefna ESB (CAP) hentar afar illa hér á landi.

citations.report_title: Þrír hópar sem myndu hagnast mest á aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu

claims.claim_text: Engin ástæða er til að ætla að sjávarútvegur og landbúnaður muni bera skarðan hlut frá borði í aðildarviðræðum við ESB.
Vöruverð á Íslandi myndi lækka við ESB-aðild vegna þess að allur innflutningur frá ESB-löndum yrði tollfrjáls.
Ótti varðandi afdrif sjávarútvegs og landbúnaðar innan ESB er uppspretta mjög umfangsmikilsm hræðsluáróðurs andstæðinga aðildar.
